title: The inevitable weakness of metrics
optimized_headline: Exploring the Hidden Limitations of Metrics in Decision-Making
raw_excerpt: There are plenty of useful things a metric can reveal. There are even more it can obscure or corrupt. It took me well over a decade of tracking my own life in ever greater detail to fully appreciate this duality, which probably reveals something about both me and the nature of measurement. Like a lot…
raw_body: There are plenty of useful things a metric can reveal. There are even more it can obscure or corrupt. It took me well over a decade of tracking my own life in ever greater detail to fully appreciate this duality, which probably reveals something about both me and the nature of measurement. Like a lot…
speedrun: Metrics can provide valuable insights but also hide important truths. After years of personal tracking, the author realized that while metrics can clarify aspects of life, they can also distort reality. For instance, focusing too much on numbers might lead to overlooking qualitative experiences. This awareness is crucial today as people increasingly rely on data, often missing the bigger picture.
why_it_matters: Individuals tracking their habits may misinterpret data, leading to skewed self-assessments and decisions.
In a data-driven world, businesses might prioritize metrics over nuanced understanding, potentially harming innovation and user experience.

lenses.eli12: Metrics can be like a map; they show you where you are, but they can also leave out important landmarks. If you only focus on the numbers, you might miss the beauty of the journey. This matters because, in everyday life, understanding the full picture helps us make better choices.
lenses.pm: For product managers, relying solely on metrics can lead to missing user needs that aren't captured in data. If a feature looks successful on paper but fails to resonate emotionally, it could lead to wasted resources. Balancing quantitative data with qualitative insights could enhance product development.
lenses.engineer: From a technical perspective, metrics often rely on specific models that can introduce biases. For example, using average values might mask outliers that indicate important trends. Understanding these limitations is key to developing more robust systems that account for both data and context.
